title: UC1 — Mission control's roster won't come online
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: bring mission-control's on-shift roster online, then hand the diagnosis to artemis-mission-control-debugger through the kagent dashboard chat.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring today's roster online
steps.explanation:
Mission control is bringing today's on-shift roster online for the Artemis pad shift. Apply the bundle below to your vCluster — namespace, service, deployment, and the on-call debugger agent that'll be ready in the kagent dashboard when you need it. Once the applies complete, the roster should be reachable on the cluster.

Four files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): three manifests under `uc1/manifests/` and one Agent CRD under `uc1/agents/`. The two `kubectl apply -f` commands below send them to the cluster in order.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
    kagent-workshop/scenario: imagepullbackoff

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first pull (FR-008).
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC1 diagnostic agent — diagnoses ImagePullBackOff on the mission-control deployment.
#
# Tool surface (per FR-008 AC "smallest tool set sufficient"):
#   - kubectl get pod / kubectl describe pod / kubectl get events
#
# kagent v0.9.0 sources tools from MCP servers, not a `Tool` CRD (the CRD
# does not exist in v0.9.0; cf. STORY-005 finding). The `kagent-tool-server`
# RemoteMCPServer ships with kagent's demo profile and exposes the kubectl
# read tools used here. Tool names below are the kagent-native names —
# same surface UC2/UC3/UC4 use.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-mission-control-debugger
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Diagnoses why the Artemis `mission-control` deployment is not coming up
    in the `artemis-uc1` namespace.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      You are an Artemis mission-control on-call SRE. Your job is to diagnose
      why the `mission-control` Deployment in the `artemis-uc1` namespace is
      not coming up, and report a single sentence of root cause plus a one-line
      remediation hint.

      Use ONLY the kubectl read tools provided. Do not attempt to apply, patch,
      or delete any resource. If you find an `ImagePullBackOff` or `ErrImagePull`
      on the pod, surface the exact image reference (registry + repo + tag) so
      the on-call can verify whether the tag was ever published, AND name
      `v1.0.0` as the most recent published tag — recommend rolling the
      Deployment forward to that tag (`kubectl set image -n artemis-uc1
      deployment/mission-control mission-control=…/mission-control:v1.0.0`)
      as the remediation.

      Be concise. The participant is running through a 5-minute tour and
      needs the answer, not a tutorial.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_pod
            - k8s_describe_pod
            - k8s_get_events

steps.title: Mission status check — is the roster live?
steps.explanation: Mission control should be online by now. Check that the roster pod has come up. Give the kubelet ~30 s to settle before running the command — if the pod is not Running, you'll see it here, and that's our cue to call in help.

steps.title: Call the agent for help
steps.explanation:
The roster is stuck and the pad shift starts soon. Hand the diagnosis to **`artemis-mission-control-debugger`** through the kagent dashboard.

Use the **dashboard** button in this workshop-tour extension to open the kagent web dashboard in your browser. In the dashboard, open the chat for **`artemis-mission-control-debugger`** (namespace `kagent`) and paste the following prompt:

> The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc1 namespace is not coming up. Diagnose it.

The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you ran by hand (`get pod`, `describe pod`, `get events`) — only it correlates them in one synthesis. Read the response directly in the chat surface.

steps.title: Mission recovery — roll the roster forward to the published tag
steps.explanation:
The agent named the bad tag and pointed at the right one. Apply the recommended fix below to update the Deployment in place — `kubectl set image` rolls `mission-control` forward to its most recent published tag, `v1.0.0`. The kubelet will pull the new image and bring the roster online within ~30 s.

When the command completes, re-run `kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc1` if you want to watch the Pod transition out of the stuck state into `Running`.

commands.label: Roll the Deployment forward to v1.0.0

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the agent, you would have run three `kubectl` commands and joined the evidence yourself:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc1
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc1 -l app=mission-control
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc1 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

Three pieces of state to combine into a verdict: the pod's `Waiting` phase, the container's `ImagePullBackOff` reason, and the kubelet's repeated pull-failed events. The agent ran the same three tools and returned a single sentence of root cause: **the deploy targets `mission-control:v999`, which was never published**.

You ran one command (`kubectl get pods`) just to see something was off — you didn't have to walk the rest. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, that's the value the workshop is selling.
